heading: TONUS TERTIUS
card_summary: This opening chapter of the Tonus Tertius (Caput I) addresses how the One (monas) unfolds into multiplicity, specifically the number twenty-seven. Zorzi follows Plato's Timaeus — mediated through Proclus and Chalcidius — in tracing the World Soul's harmonic structure through doublings and triplings of the monad up to 27, the cube of the first number (3). The interpreters Proclus and Chalcidius substitute six (the first perfect number) for unity, extending the series to 162, then fill the intervals with diapente, diatessaron, tone, semitone, and leimma harmonics. Zorzi also invokes the Hebrew concept of materia prima — called Hivili or Tohu by the Hebrews, Chaos by the poets, and Hyle by the Greeks — which precedes numerical creation just as the supreme monad precedes number, and notes that Moses wisely described this formless substrate before the work of creation.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay significantly with deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with source materials, and comprehensive explanations of referenced concepts. I'll preserve all blockquotes, citations, and formatting while adding substantial new content.

## Central Argument

The opening chapter of the Tonus Tertius poses the cosmological question of how the One (monas) diffuses itself into multiplicity without losing its essential unity. Zorzi's answer is harmonic proportion: the monad extends itself through the ratios of Platonic musical harmony, arriving at the number twenty-seven — the cube of three — as the structural limit of this diffusion. This approach represents a sophisticated synthesis of Platonic cosmology, Pythagorean harmonic theory, and Neoplatonic emanationism, all interpreted through the lens of Christian Kabbalah. The chapter's significance lies not merely in its arithmetic schema but in its demonstration that creation itself operates according to musical and mathematical laws, a vision that underwrites the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*.

## The Platonic Framework: Timaeus and the Number 27

Zorzi grounds the discussion explicitly in the *Timaeus*, noting that Plato followed Timaeus Locrus in constructing the World Soul through a series of doublings and triplings from the monad:

> Plato Timaeum Locrum imitatus animae, et mundi fabricam describit per acceptam monadem, et per duplatas, triplatas, et quadriplatas proportiones usque ad viginti et septem, cubum videlicet primi numeri devenit, addens omnia intervalla repleta fuisse.

<cite>p. 410</cite>

The series 1–2–3–4–9–8–27 (the Platonic lambda) yields 27 as the cube of 3, identified here as "cubum primi numeri" — the cube of the first (odd) number. This is the structural scaffolding of both the World Soul and the cosmos as a whole.

The *Timaeus* passage (35b–36b) that Zorzi invokes describes how the Demiurge constructs the World Soul by taking a measured portion of Being, Sameness, and Difference, then filling the mixture with harmonic ratios. Plato's construction proceeds by alternating doublings and triplings: starting from 1, the doubling series produces 1–2–4–8, while the tripling series produces 1–3–9–27. The intervals between these terms are then filled with geometric means, creating a continuous harmonic progression. This mathematical construction is not arbitrary metaphor but, for both Plato and Zorzi, a representation of how divine reason (nous) structures the cosmos through rational proportion. The World Soul, constructed according to these ratios, becomes the animating principle through which the sensible cosmos participates in the eternal Forms.

Zorzi's adoption of this Platonic framework signals his conviction that Renaissance Neoplatonism can absorb the technical rigor of medieval scholasticism while recovering the ontological depth of ancient philosophy. The *Timaeus* was available to medieval thinkers primarily through the partial Latin translation by Chalcidius (c. 4th century), whose commentary became the authoritative medieval reading. However, by Zorzi's era, the full Platonic text was being recovered and translated anew, allowing for more direct engagement with Plato's original reasoning. Zorzi's invocation of "Timaeus Locrus" (the ancient Pythagorean whose work Plato claims to follow) suggests a historical consciousness: he recognizes that the harmonic ratios derive ultimately from Pythagorean mathematical mysticism, which understood number as the fundamental principle of reality. This heritage connects Platonic cosmology to the older Pythagorean conviction that all things are numbers and that mathematical relationships underlie manifest phenomena.

The number 27 itself carries profound significance in Zorzi's system. As the cube of 3, it represents a completed manifestation of the ternary principle—the holy trinity of Christian theology, reimagined as the fundamental ordering principle of creation. The progression from 1 (the monad) through 3 (the first odd number) to 9 (3 squared) to 27 (3 cubed) traces a path of manifestation in which the divine unity progressively unfolds into visible multiplicity while maintaining harmonic coherence. Twenty-seven becomes the maximum extension of the monad before multiplicity becomes excessive and loses the unity that characterizes authentic cosmic order. This is why Zorzi immediately turns to the distributions of intellects, heavens, and elements, all of which can be mapped onto this ninefold and twenty-sevenfold structure.

## Proclus and Chalcidius: The Senarius as Substitute Unity

Zorzi then turns to the major ancient commentators on the *Timaeus* — Proclus and Chalcidius, along with later interpreters ("posteri") — who make a technical substitution: instead of beginning the harmonic series from 1 (the monad itself), they place six (the first perfect number) in the position of unity:

> senarium primum perfectum numerum unitatis loco collocant, quem eodem ordine duplando, triplandoque usque ad 162 deveniunt. Et tunc intervalla replent diapentica, diatessaronica harmonia, tono etiam, semitonio, et lemmate, ut tota anima, totusque mundus harmonicus sit, et omnem contineat divinam harmoniam.

<cite>p. 410</cite>

This produces the extended series culminating in 162, which is then filled with the standard intervals of Greek musical theory: the diapente (fifth), diatessaron (fourth), the tone (whole step), the semitone, and the leimma (the small Pythagorean remainder). The goal is a fully harmonic cosmos — "tota anima, totusque mundus harmonicus" — in which every level of being participates in divine proportion.

The substitution of six for one represents a crucial interpretive decision in the reception history of Platonic cosmology. The number six holds special status in Pythagorean and Neoplatonic mathematics: it is the first perfect number, equal to the sum of its proper divisors (1 + 2 + 3 = 6). In Christian theology, the number six also carries significance as the day of creation when God made the human being "in his image" (Genesis 1:27). By installing six as a kind of operative unity within the harmonic series, the medieval and Renaissance commentators were able to maintain fidelity to Plato's original harmonic framework while also extending the cosmic order to encompass more elements and degrees of being than the simpler 1–2–4–8 and 1–3–9–27 series allowed.

Proclus of Lycia (412–485 CE), the most influential Neoplatonic philosopher in the Christian tradition, developed in his *Commentary on the Timaeus* an elaborate harmonic cosmology that Zorzi draws upon. Proclus understood the World Soul not as a passive receptacle but as an active principle of mediation and transformation, linked to a graded hierarchy of divine beings. The extension from 27 to 162 (which equals 27 × 6, or 2⁷ × 3⁴) permits a more nuanced mapping of these hierarchical degrees. Similarly, Chalcidius, whose commentary remained the primary medieval access point to the *Timaeus*, offered his own account of harmonic intervals and numerical structure, emphasizing the continuity between the mathematical principles governing the World Soul and the visible cosmos.

When Zorzi adopts this extended series with the senarius as the pivot point, he is thus positioning himself within a centuries-long interpretive tradition while claiming the authority of both Platonic philosophy and Christian Neoplatonism. The "later interpreters" (posteri) he mentions likely include medieval thinkers such as Macrobius and Islamic philosophers like Al-Ghazali, who also grappled with harmonizing Platonic cosmology with monotheistic theology. Zorzi's synthesis goes further by explicitly incorporating Hebrew Kabbalah, which operates according to its own numerical and harmonic principles—a move that required considerable hermeneutical finesse to justify.

The filling of intervals with musical ratios—diapentica, diatessaronica, tonus, semitonium, and lemma—grounds the abstract arithmetic in the concrete realm of Pythagorean acoustics. The diapente (perfect fifth, 3:2 ratio) and diatessaron (perfect fourth, 4:3 ratio) are the primary consonances; between them lies the tone (9:8 ratio), the smallest interval in standard Greek theory; below the tone sit the semitone and the leimma. By filling the gaps in the numerical series with these precise musical ratios, the cosmos becomes literally music—a "harmonic cosmos" in which every created thing resonates in mathematical proportion to every other. This is not metaphor for Zorzi; it is the fundamental nature of reality.

## The Ternary and the Nine

Zorzi introduces a compressed cosmological schema based on the number three:

> Nam primus, et summus monas in primum numerum, ternarium videlicet diffunditur, qui si quadretur novenarium complet. Quo numero intellectuum separatorum ordines, coeli, elementariaque omnia summa harmonia distribuuntur.

<cite>p. 410–411</cite>

The supreme monad first projects into three (the ternary, the "primum numerum"), which when squared yields nine. This ninefold structure organizes the orders of separated intellects (angels), the heavens, and the elemental world — a preview of distributions Zorzi promises to demonstrate more fully below. When the nine is further cubed, it returns to the twenty-seven already established. The progression 1 → 3 → 9 → 27 encodes the entire cosmological hierarchy in a single arithmetic movement.

This ternary progression embodies several crucial theological principles. The three-fold structure naturally invites identification with the Christian Trinity—God the Father (monad, source), God the Son (the differentiation into otherness, the "primum numerum"), and the Holy Spirit (the principle of unity-in-multiplicity that allows the nine and the twenty-seven to maintain harmonic coherence). However, Zorzi's deployment of this structure is sufficiently subtle that it can also accommodate Neoplatonic triadic structures: the One, Intellect, and Soul; or Being, Sameness, and Difference from Plato's *Timaeus*. This doctrinal flexibility is characteristic of Christian Kabbalah—a philosophy designed to reveal hidden harmonies between seemingly incompatible traditions.

The squared ternary yielding nine is particularly significant. If the three represents the first differentiation of the monad, then squaring it (3 × 3 = 9) represents the multiplication or distribution of this principle across multiple domains. The nine separated intellects, the nine heavens (or their subdivisions), and the nine elementary categories can thus all be understood as manifestations of the same divine principle operating at different levels of reality. This is why Zorzi can move so freely between domains—angelic hierarchies, celestial spheres, and elemental compositions—all fit the same nine-fold or twenty-seven-fold template. Unity is preserved through harmonic proportion even amid manifest diversity.

The progression from monad through ternary and nine to twenty-seven describes a kind of cosmic unfoldment in which being becomes increasingly determinate and particularized. Yet at each stage, Zorzi insists, the underlying harmony remains intact. The twenty-seven is not a descent into chaos but a final differentiation that still bears the imprint of the monad's unity. This is essential to Zorzi's vision: creation is not a fall away from divine perfection but rather the perfect expression of divine wisdom in sensible form. The cosmos is not damaged or compromised; it is the harmonious outpouring of God's infinite fecundity.

## Materia Prima: Chaos, Hyle, Tohu

Before the harmonic distribution of things can proceed, Zorzi introduces the concept of materia prima — the formless substrate that logically precedes creation. In a characteristic trilingual move, he gives this concept in three traditions:

> materia prima (quam poetae Chaos, Graeci Hyle, et Hebraei [Hivili vel Tohu] id est Hivili vel Tohu dicunt) praeexigebatur, quae cum numeratis non connumeraretur, sicut agens primum, et summus monas in aliorum numerum non venit, quamvis per unitatem suam omnibus praestet, ut numerentur.

<cite>p. 411</cite>

The equation is precise: materia prima stands to the created numerical order exactly as the supreme monad stands above all number. Neither is "counted among" the things it makes possible. The poets call it Chaos; the Greeks call it Hyle (matter); the Hebrew term given — rendered in transliterated form as "Hivili" and "Tohu" — corresponds to the biblical *tohu wa-bohu* of Genesis 1:2, the formless void before creation. The Hebrew term carries the technical kabbalistic resonance of unformed potentiality.

Zorzi's invocation of materia prima reveals his engagement with the entire medieval philosophical tradition. The Aristotelian tradition, mediated through Aquinas and Scotus, understood matter (hyle) as principle of potentiality, correlative to form as principle of actuality. Prime matter (materia prima) was the logical limit of this principle—matter completely bereft of any form whatsoever, existing as pure potentiality. Such matter has no being in itself; it exists only in relation to form, just as potentiality exists only in relation to actuality. This Aristotelian framework was dominant in scholasticism, but it posed difficulties for Christian theology: if matter is the principle of passivity and potentiality, how can the creation of the material world be compatible with God's absolute perfection and actuality?

The Platonic and Neoplatonic traditions offered an alternative vision. In Plato's *Timaeus*, the Receptacle (chora) is described as a kind of space or medium that receives all sensible forms—a "third kind" beyond being and becoming. It is often compared to gold, which can take any shape without being fundamentally altered. This receptive principle is not identical to Aristotelian matter but operates similarly: it is the substrate in which forms actualize themselves sensibly. The Neoplatonists, particularly Plotinus, refined this picture by understanding matter as the lowest emanation from the One—the limit of the One's creative outpouring. At this limit, reality becomes so attenuated that it approaches non-being; but because it proceeds from the One, it retains a vestige of intelligibility and order.

The Hebrew concept of tohu va-bohu (*tohu* = formless, void; *bohu* = emptiness, chaos) from Genesis 1:2 described the state of the cosmos before God's creative acts brought forth determinate things. Kabbalists, building on rabbinic exegesis, interpreted this not as matter in the Aristotelian sense but as the lowest grade of divine manifestation—the threshold where the infinite (Ein Sof) becomes knowable through finite determinations. In this view, tohu is not absolutely non-being (which would be incompatible with God's omnipresence and the doctrine that "there is nothing beside Him") but rather the undifferentiated plenum of divine potentiality before the emergence of distinct things (yesh).

Zorzi's synthesis brings all three traditions into alignment. The poets' Chaos, the Greek Hyle, and the Hebrew Tohu all point to the same underlying reality: the receptive, unformed principle that must logically exist before any created thing can take shape. And crucially, this principle stands to creation as the monad stands to number. Just as the monad is not itself counted as a number but is the source from which all number proceeds, materia prima is not itself numbered among created things but is the condition of possibility for their numerical determination. The analogy is precise and profound: both monad and materia prima operate beyond the created order while making that order possible.

This introduces a subtle but critical point for Zorzi's metaphysics. In the Neoplatonic hierarchy, there is a danger of an infinite descent from the One through multiple levels of reality, terminating in matter as the absolute nadir. Plotinus argued vigorously against the view that matter is evil or absolutely opposed to the divine; instead, he saw it as the final emanation, necessary for the full actualization of the divine creativity. Zorzi's parallel between monad and materia prima suggests a similar vision: matter is not opposed to the divine principle but is rather the final expression of it. The cosmos unfolds from the monad through a harmonic series culminating in the material realm, and matter itself, as the receptive principle underlying all sensible things, partakes of the divine order. This is why the entire cosmos can be described as harmonic—from the highest separated intellects down to the elements and minerals.

## Moses and the Wisdom of Genesis

The section closes with a brief but pointed observation: Moses, described as "sapientissime," described this formless matter *before* the account of creation — implicitly aligning the Mosaic text with the philosophical insight that an unformed substrate must precede all numerical and harmonic differentiation. This move harmonizes Platonic cosmology with the Hebrew scriptural tradition, a signature gesture of Zorzi's Christian Kabbalist synthesis.

The strategic placement of this observation cannot be overstated. By attributing wisdom to Moses in explicitly recognizing the priority of the formless void, Zorzi claims scriptural authority for his philosophical cosmology. He is not merely importing pagan Platonism into Christian theology; rather, he is arguing that Moses, divinely inspired, already understood and revealed the truth that Greek philosophy later rediscovered through reason. This hermeneutical move—finding in Scripture confirmation of philosophical truths—was central to the apologetic strategy of the entire Christian Neoplatonic and Kabbalist tradition from the Church Fathers forward.

Genesis 1:2 reads: "And the earth was without form and void (*tohu va-bohu*); and darkness was upon the face of the deep; and the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters." The medieval Jewish philosopher Moses Maimonides had already argued in his *Guide for the Perplexed* that the tohu va-bohu described in Genesis is not created matter in the temporal sense but rather the primordial condition on which all temporal creation depends. Zorzi, writing nearly four centuries later, is drawing on this kabbalistic and medieval rationalist tradition. By attributing to Moses not merely a narrative account of creation but a philosophical understanding of the logical priority of materia prima, Zorzi elevates Scripture to the status of philosophical wisdom.

Furthermore, the observation that Moses describes the formless matter *before* describing the work of creation (Genesis 1:2 precedes the acts of creation in Genesis 1:3 onwards) performs an important rhetorical function. It suggests that the order of the text mirrors the logical and ontological order of reality: potentiality must be established before actuality can emerge from it; the receptive principle must be recognized before the creative acts that fill it with determinate forms. This is an instance of Zorzi's broader strategy of finding in the Mosaic text an implicit philosophical framework that aligns with both Greek philosophy and Hebrew Kabbalah.

The specific designation of materia prima as Hivili or Tohu in Hebrew shows Zorzi's direct familiarity with Hebrew texts—not merely mediated through translations but engaged with directly. The term Hivili (likely a transliteration of *Hevel* or related to *Hovle*, void) is less common than Tohu, suggesting that Zorzi consulted multiple Hebrew sources or variant traditions within Jewish exegesis. His precision in these details indicates that *De Harmonia Mundi* is not merely a philosophical synthesis but a genuinely learned work engaging primary sources across multiple languages and traditions.

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter establishes the arithmetic and harmonic backbone of the Tonus Tertius. The movement from monad to ternary to nine to twenty-seven provides the quantitative skeleton onto which all subsequent distributions — of angelic orders, celestial spheres, elements, and created beings — will be mapped. The chapter also performs Zorzi's characteristic method: Plato and his Greek commentators (Proclus, Chalcidius) are read as corroborating both the Mosaic account and the kabbalistic understanding of pre-creational formlessness. The technical musical vocabulary (diapentica, diatessaronica, tonus, semitonium, lemma) anchors the metaphysics in precise Pythagorean proportionality, making the cosmos literally a harmonic structure.

Beyond the purely doctrinal content, this opening chapter stakes a claim about the nature of philosophical investigation itself. Zorzi is not presenting three separate traditions (Platonic, Hebraic, Christian) that happen to overlap. Rather, he is arguing that all three traditions, properly understood, express a single underlying reality—that the cosmos is fundamentally ordered according to harmonic proportion, that this order flows from a transcendent unity (the monad), and that the sensible world, far from being a degraded shadow of the intelligible realm, is the perfect manifestation of divine wisdom in material form.

This vision has profound implications for Renaissance thought. It challenges both the extreme Neoplatonic despair toward matter that had infected medieval theology and the mechanical philosophy that would later characterize early modern science. For Zorzi, the material cosmos is sacred precisely because it embodies divine harmony. To study the natural world is thus a form of theology—an investigation of how the infinite expresses itself in the finite, how the monad manifests itself in multiplicity, how the One maintains its unity through all the diversities of creation.

The numerical and harmonic schemas introduced here—the ternary, the nine, the twenty-seven—will reappear throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi* in increasingly concrete and specific applications. The angelic hierarchies will be organized in ninefold and twenty-sevenfold structures; the planetary spheres will exhibit these same proportions; the human soul will be shown to recapitulate the same harmonic order. By establishing the abstract arithmetic at the outset, Zorzi prepares the ground for showing that every aspect of creation—from the highest intelligences to the lowest material elements—participates in a single unified harmonic cosmos. This is the grand claim that motivates the entire work: that *De Harmonia Mundi* is not a merely decorative philosophy but a true account of how reality is structured, from its source in divine unity to its manifestation in sensible multiplicity.
